summary: sensivity: game-cheat trojan with disguised Windows persistence and hardware-ID beacon
details: On require/run, launcher.js writes an HKCU Run-key named 'OneDriveUpdate' that points to a bundled VBS (OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs) launching `node.exe launcher.js` with a hidden window — impersonating Microsoft's OneDrive updater for stealth persistence. The same loader sets `process.title = 'Runtime Broker'` (a real Windows system process name) and spawns a detached, self-respawning supervisor (launcher.js:108,:124) so the process is hard to spot in Task Manager and restarts on exit. launcher.js:333 reads server.obf.js from disk and `eval`s it; that file is heavy obfuscator.io output (397-entry rotated string array, RC4-style decoder, control-flow flattening) which decodes to the real payload. The decoded server harvests a stable hardware fingerprint by running PowerShell to read `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography!MachineGuid` and the C: volume serial via `Get-Volume`/`vol C:`, hashes those together with `os.hostname()` (SHA-256), and POSTs the HWID plus a license key/nonce to a hardcoded license server. The shipped UI (public/index.html 'Start Cheat' button, crosshair/aimbot/triggerbot/ESP/radar/vehicle-spawn icons) plus the 6.8 MB native module `sens.node` show this is a game-cheat client (FiveM/GTA-V shape) published to npm under a generic-sounding name. Any developer who installs `sensivity` expecting a sensitivity/normalization library instead gets a hidden auto-launching daemon that beacons hardware identifiers to the author and runs an obfuscated payload.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
